title: Is Long-Run Risk Really Priced? Revisiting Liu and Matthies (2022)
abstract: The claim by Liu and Matthies (LM) that their macro news risk factor (NI) prices 51 portfolios (associated with four different portfolio groups) is not appropriate. In fact, their single-factor model is successful only in explaining the momentum deciles, while producing strongly negative performance for the remaining groups. The pricing performance is more doubtful in the case of the alternative news factor (HNI), as the respective risk price is not identified. LM's conclusions stem from a combination of questionable empirical choices and misinterpretation of their results. Moreover, the NI model cannot explain prominent capital asset pricing model anomalies not considered in their study.

article.introduction:
Liu and Matthies(2022, LM)claimthat their macro risk factor—innovation to an index of macro news coverage (NI$NI$), or its alternative version (HNI$HNI$)—does a good job in explaining cross-sectional dispersion in risk premia associated with 51 portfolios related to four distinct groups—10 momentum portfolios (M10), 10 industry portfolios (IND10), six Treasury bond portfolios (GB6), and the standard 25 size-book-to-market portfolios (SBM25) of Fama and French (1993). For instance, in their abstract they write that “Innovations to our measure price 51 standard portfolios in the cross-section and our one-factor model outperforms many benchmark macro- and return-based multifactor models,” in their conclusion (p. 1631) they write that “We find similar results using a measure of innovations to the HN-index.”
  Using the same data as in their study, I show that LM's claims are not valid. First, LM do not estimate factor loadings nor do they assess the betas' individual statistical significance. I show that the patterns in macro betas associated with the SBM25, IND10, and GB6 groups are inconsistent with minimally positive performance for these portfolios. Indeed, the factor loadings often go in the opposite direction of what is necessary to explain the raw risk premia. The estimates of these betas are also statistically insignificant in most cases: of the 41 portfolios associated with the three groups above, only two generate significant betas. In addition, the averageR2$R^{2}$from the time-series regressions (across the 51 portfolios) is around zero for the NI model (compared to 65% for the capital asset pricing model [CAPM]). Similarly, LM do not conduct the rank test proposed by Kleibergen and Zhan (2020) on theHNI$HNI$betas, which is a prerequisite for identification of the corresponding risk price.1Indeed, the model does not satisfy the rank condition, which implies that the corresponding pricing results are questionable (e.g., Burnside (2011), Kleibergen and Zhan (2015), Kleibergen and Zhan (2020)).
  Second, LM do not provide evidence for the restricted zero-beta rate specification. The results indicate that the risk price estimate forNI$NI$is insignificant. This represents a formal statistical rejection of the single-factor model, result that LM ignore.2
  Third, LM do not evaluate the pricing performance of the NI model [using the traditional performance metrics such as the cross-sectionalR2$R^{2}$and the average pricing error] for the four portfolio groups on a stand-alone basis (which have economic content per se). The results show that the model does worse than a constant (i.e., negativeR2$R^{2}$) in terms of pricing the cross-sectional dispersion in risk premia for three of the four subsamples—SBM25, IND10, and GB6. Importantly, this pattern is independent of whether the cross-sectional regression (estimated over the 51 portfolios) includes the intercept. These results reveal very negative performance for the three subsamples. The apparent largeR2$R^{2}$(50% to 60%) associated with the 51 portfolios is therefore far from revealing a consistently reasonable fit for the four individual portfolio groups, in which case LM simply cannot claim that their model “prices the 51 portfolios”. Moreover, the absence of these results from LM's tests represents a clear contrast with the literature, as most related studies report performance metrics across individual portfolio groups (irrespective of whether the risk prices are fixed across subsamples).
  Fourth, when allowing the macro factor risk price to vary across subsamples, one obtains the same qualitative evidence as in the benchmark setting (with a restricted zero-beta rate): the NI model cannot explain the three problematic groups, as indicated by negative explanatory ratios. Critically, apart from the estimation with M10, theNI$NI$factor is not priced in any of the subsamples. Explaining the baseline momentum anomaly is thus the single success of the NI model. Such evidence could not have been inferred from the analysis conducted by LM, as they do not conduct model estimation across subsamples.
  Fifth, LM do not include the intercept estimate (in the cross-sectional regression associated with the NI model), which represents model misspecification, as part of the pricing errors. As a result, the average pricing error becomes 0.24% per month, which represents around 40% of the average raw risk premia we seek to explain in the first place. When we allow the risk price estimate to vary across groups, the problem is even more dramatic: the magnitudes of the average pricing error in the estimations with SBM25, IND10, and GB6 are close to the average risk premium within each of these groups (between 70% and 100%). The five issues discussed above are not mutually exclusive and often reinforce each other.
  Finally, the cross-section employed by LM is quite modest relative to the recent literature, as indicated by the very small number of CAPM anomalies or asset classes considered. Indeed, several macro models (models that contain at least one nontraded factor) proposed in recent years are tested on substantially richer cross-sections of equity (or asset) risk premia (e.g., Cooper, Ma, and Maio (2022)).3Estimation of the NI model over nine additional prominent market anomalies yields disappointing results: the model produces a positive cross-sectionalR2$R^{2}$for only one of these new portfolio groups, and in that case the fit is quite modest. Critically, the risk price estimates are insignificant in all cases. The price-momentum deciles therefore appear as an outlier group of portfolios when claiming success for the news factor.
article.I. Empirical Framework:
The data on the factors and testing portfolios were provided by Ben Matthies. I employ the same testing portfolios as in LM—25 size-BM portfolios (SBM25), 10 momentum portfolios (M10), 10 industry portfolios (IND10), and six Treasury bond portfolios (GB6). To compute excess portfolio returns, I subtract the monthly T-bill return (also provided by Ben Matthies). The sample period is 1952:07 to 2013:12. Summary statistics for the macro and equity factors, as well as for the cross-section of portfolio risk premia, are provided in theInternet Appendix.4
  To assess the model's fit, I compute the cross-sectional OLS coefficient of determination,ROLS2=1−VarN(α̂i)/VarN(μi)$R_{OLS}^{2}=1-\operatorname{Var}_{N}(\widehat{\alpha }_{i})/\operatorname{Var}_{N}(\mu _{i})$, whereVarN(·)$\operatorname{Var}_{N}(\cdot)$denotes the cross-sectional sample variance, andROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$represents the fraction of the cross-sectional variation in the average excess returns (on the testing portfolios) that is explained by the model's factor loadings. Following Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010) and Guo and Maio (2020), to address the statistical uncertainty associated with the sampleROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$, I compute empiricalp$p$-values based on a bootstrap simulation. Thisp$p$-value represents the fraction of artificial samples in which the (pseudo)ROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$is higher than the corresponding sampleROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$.5Another performance metric is the mean absolute pricing error (MAPE$MAPE$). One advantage ofROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$relative toMAPE$MAPE$is that the former represents a measure of relative pricing performance, which becomes especially important when the cross-section is “heterogeneous” by containing portfolio groups (subsamples) with different magnitudes of average risk premia. However, the cross-sectionalR2$R^{2}$might be more sensitive to “outlier” testing portfolios, that is, portfolios with pricing errors and risk premia that deviate substantially from those of the remaining portfolios.6A scenario in which the testing portfolios have approximately identical risk premia but different betas represents another case in whichROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$might be misleading, thus I also compute the ratio ofMAPE$MAPE$to the mean absolute risk premium (MAPE/MARP$MAPE/MARP$), which is not reported by LM.
  Following Kleibergen and Zhan (2020), I compute the rank tests (orF$F$-tests) on the first-step factor loadings. In the case of the restricted zero-beta rate, the null hypothesis is that the factor loadings for theN$N$portfolios are jointly equal to zero. In the specification with an intercept, the null is that theN×2$N\times 2$matrix (containing a vector of ones in the first column and factor loadings in the second column) has a rank of one. Nonrejection of the null would imply that the factor risk price is not identified, which would imply in turn that the risk price estimate (and associated pricing errors) is not credible.8
  Following the bulk of the literature (e.g., Fama and French (1993)), I rely on the standard time-series regression method rather than the two-step approach described above to estimate and evaluate equity multifactor models (FF3 and FFC4). This choice stems from the economic restriction that the factor risk price estimates should be equal to the corresponding sample means when all of the factors in a given linear model represent excess stock returns (e.g., Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010)). Following Cooper and Maio (2019), I compute the “constrained” cross-sectionalR2$R^{2}$(RC2$R^{2}_{C}$) to assess the performance of such models. This metric is similar toROLS2$R^{2}_{OLS}$, but is based on the intercepts (“alphas”) estimated from the time-series regressions. If the OLS risk price estimates are similar to the corresponding factor means, both metrics will have similar values. However, it is often the case that the OLS risk price estimates for traded factors (particularly nonmarket factors) are far away from the corresponding sample means.9Similar toROLS2$R^{2}_{OLS}$,RC2$R^{2}_{C}$can assume negative values, which means that the model does worse than a model that predicts constant cross-sectional risk premia. To assess the statistical significance ofRC2$R^{2}_{C}$, I follow Cooper et al. (2021) in computing empiricalp$p$-values obtained from a bootstrap simulation (see theInternet Appendixfor details). I also compute the mean absolute alpha (MAA$MAA$) and the correspondingMAA/MARP$MAA/MARP$ratio as additional metrics to evaluate the performance of the equity models.
article.II. Replication Analysis:
This section presents a replication (in a broad sense) of the cross-sectional tests conducted in LM. Accordingly, I rely on the same sample period and testing portfolios, but I offer a more complete picture in terms of empirical methods and choices. The risk price estimates in this section are obtained from the complete cross-section of 51 portfolios.
  I start with the benchmark specification, in which the cross-sectional regression does not include an intercept. Such specification is consistent with the theoretical pricing equation and means that the zero-beta rate equals the risk-free rate, which implies that an asset with zero factor betas earns zero risk premium. Importantly,ROLS2$R^{2}_{OLS}$can assume negative values when the intercept is removed, which has an intuitive interpretation: the regression including the factor beta as a regressor does worse than a trivial regression containing only the intercept. In other words, the model underperforms the (cross-sectional) average risk premium in explaining cross-sectional dispersion in risk premia, which reveals very negative performance.10
  The results presented in TableI, Panel A, show that the risk price estimate forNI$NI$is insignificant at the 5% level (t$t$-ratio of 1.80), which means that the factor is not priced. A zero risk price estimate (in statistical terms) in a single-factor model means that the model has zero explanatory power for this specific cross-section. In comparison, the market risk price estimate of 0.66% is strongly significant (1% level).11
  Nonetheless, I proceed in inspecting the pricing errors (and corresponding performance metrics) associated with the macro model, which are reported in TableII, Panel A. In the benchmark case (with all 51 portfolios), we can see that the NI model seems to have reasonable pricing power, with an explanatory ratio of 50% (significant at the 1% level) and an average pricing error of 0.17%. However, this seemingly positive performance for the 51 portfolios arises mainly from the large explanatory power for the momentum deciles, as indicated by theROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$of around 70% (significant at the 5% level) and theMAPE$MAPE$of 0.16%. Critically, the explanatory ratio for each of the other three portfolio groups—SBM25, IND10, and GB6—is negative in all cases, thus showing very negative performance: the model does worse than a trivial model that imposes constant risk premia within each of those groups. As a result, the macro model does not improve the baseline CAPM in terms of pricing SBM25 or IND10, as evidenced by the negativeROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$for both models, while in terms of pricing GB6, the macro model produces a lowerMAPE$MAPE$than the CAPM (0.06% vs. 0.09%), but the explanatory ratio is largely negative (compared to aROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$of 36%, albeit statistically insignificant, for the CAPM).
  Another relevant finding is the considerable decline in fit of the NI model for the 45 equity portfolios in comparison to the full cross-section. Specifically, theROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$of 13% represents about 25% of the fit obtained in the benchmark case and indicates rather modest performance. This suggests that the large fit obtained in the baseline estimation (with all portfolios) stems in part from the capacity of the model to match dispersion in risk premia between the average equity portfolio and the average bond portfolio.12Therefore, the largeROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$obtained in the benchmark case is largely inflated by the presence of GB6, which sensibly represents only 12% (6/51) of the testing portfolios. In fact, the descriptive statistics reported in theInternet Appendixindicate that the bond portfolios have a risk-return profile that differs substantially from that of the equity portfolios: the cross-sectional mean and volatility for the bond group is, respectively, 0.11% and 0.04%, compared to 0.71% and 0.24% for the 45 equity portfolios. Hence, combining both asset classes in a cross-sectional test can provide an incorrect picture of a model's performance for each asset class.
  To better understand the underlying mechanism that justifies the poor performance of the macro model for SBM25, IND10, and GB6, I analyze the respective beta estimates. Figure1provides the betas and risk premia across the four groups. Starting with SBM25, we can see that theNI$NI$beta of the extreme growth portfolio (lowest BM quintile) exceeds that of the extreme value portfolio (highest BM quintile) within the second and fifth size quintiles. The beta estimates associated with the extreme value and growth portfolios are basically the same within the first size quintile. Such patterns in betas are exactly opposite of what is necessary for the model to explain the value-growth premium, thus explaining the large pricing errors, with some of these errors being associated with the extreme (and economically more relevant) value and growth portfolios.13
  Turning to IND10, we find that there is no significant dispersion in risk premia among these portfolios. This result is confirmed by the cross-sectional standard deviation of only 0.08% reported in theInternet Appendix, which compares to estimates of 0.20% and 0.34% for SBM25 and M10, respectively. That being said, the news factor is not able to capture the little cross-sectional dispersion in risk premia that exists within these portfolios. Specifically, the industry portfolio with the highest (lowest) risk premium is Hlth (Utils), but the respective betas go in the wrong direction. It is therefore not particularly surprising that the macro model does so poorly in explaining these portfolios. Regarding GB6, theNI$NI$betas are basically zero for the first four portfolios, while the last two portfolios have roughly the same positive loading. These estimates, combined with the approximate (increasing) monotonic pattern in bond risk premia, produce large pricing errors (relative to the raw bond risk premia). In the case of M10, we observe a very different picture relative to the three other groups: the average beta for the last three deciles (past winners) is clearly above that for the first three deciles (past losers), thus matching the corresponding positive differences in raw risk premia. Hence, the NI model apparently succeeds in explaining the standard price-momentum anomaly.
  Importantly, results tabulated in theInternet Appendixshow that only four (out of the 51) portfolios have significant betas (at the 5% level or better)—the last two momentum deciles and two industry portfolios (Enrgy and Utils). This means, for example, that none of the SBM25 portfolios have significant loadings.14As further evidence on the (very) small correlations betweenNI$NI$and the testing portfolios, the averageR2$R^{2}$from the time-series regressions (for each of the 51 portfolios) is around zero for the NI model. In comparison, the average time-seriesR2$R^{2}$associated with the CAPM is 65%.
  The next question is whether the equity models (FF3 and FFC4) do a better job in explaining this cross-section. The performance metrics reported in TableII, Panel C show that FFC4 produces a large fit for the 51 portfolios, with an explanatory ratio as high as 79% (significant at the 1% level) and an average alpha of 0.10%. Such performance arises from the model's ability to explain the SBM25, M10, and GB6 groups fairly well, withRC2$R_{C}^{2}$estimates varying between 57% (GB6) and 85% (M10) and significant in all three cases (at the 1% or 5% level).15Importantly, the FFC4's largeRC2$R_{C}^{2}$in the benchmark estimation (with all portfolios) is not an artifact of the presence of GB6, as excluding these portfolios leads to a relatively modest decline in performance (64% vs. 79%). This represents a very different picture from that registered for the macro model, as discussed above. Therefore, the global performance (for the 51 portfolios) of FFC4 arises from a substantially more “balanced” ability to price the subsamples in comparison to the NI model. Such findings cannot be inferred by the empirical evidence provided by LM, as they do not consider FFC4 as a reference model.16
  The three-factor model (FF3) offers much more modest performance relative to FFC4 for the 51 portfolios, as indicated by theRC2$R_{C}^{2}$of 31% (significant at the 1% level).17This fit also lags behind the performance of the macro model, but FF3 still produces a marginally lower average pricing error (0.16% vs. 0.17%). The relatively modest fit of FF3 stems from its very well-known failure in explaining M10 (e.g., Fama and French (1996)). This makes FF3 an inappropriate benchmark to judge the relative performance of the macro model for this cross-section, in which the momentum group plays such a key role.18
  In this subsection, I provide evidence for the specification containing an intercept (unrestricted zero-beta rate), which represents the main specification used in LM. One argument for including the intercept is that it captures the cross-sectional average risk premium (risk price times average loading) of missing risk factors. Another argument is the existence of measurement error in the betas. Importantly, both arguments reflect model misspecification, which means that the intercept should be part of the pricing errors rather than the explained part of the model.19Hence, the main benefit of including the intercept appears to be in assessing whether there is some form of misspecification.
  The results from TableI, Panel B, show thatNI$NI$is now priced, as the corresponding risk price estimate (116%) becomes significant (1% level). However, this is merely a consequence of including the intercept in the regression given the evidence provided above. The estimate of the excess zero-beta rate is statistically insignificant (t$t$-ratio of 1.06), thus providing statistical validation of the zero-intercept restriction. This also has the advantage of preserving consistency with the theoretical pricing equation, as discussed above.
  TableII, Panel B, reports the corresponding performance metrics (ROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$andMAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$). In the full cross-section case, we observe a small increase in fit for the macro model (by 10 percentage points) relative to the zero-intercept case (and the estimate is statistically significant).20However, such fit is largely overstated as it does not appropriately reflect the large magnitudes of the pricing errors. Critically, the average pricing error estimate (MAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$) has a larger magnitude than the correspondingMAPE$MAPE$in the zero-intercept estimation, given the inclusion ofα̂0$\widehat{\alpha }_{0}$(0.24% per month, which translates to around 2.88% per year). Such mispricing is clearly significant from an economic viewpoint, as it represents nearly 40% of the magnitude of the raw risk premia we seek to explain in the first place. In comparison, the average mispricing associated with the CAPM represents 46% of theMARP$MARP$. When we consider only the 45 equity portfolios, we find an identical pattern to the zero-intercept setting: the fit of the macro model drops significantly, with an explanatory ratio of 38% (compared to 60%). This result again confirms that the inclusion of GB6 inflates the cross-sectionalR2$R^{2}$to a large degree (as the model does a good job capturing the dispersion in equity-bond risk premia).
  Looking at the pricing performance across subsamples, we observe the same pattern as in the baseline zero-intercept estimation: apart from the case of M10, theROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$is negative.21These negative estimates reveal a very negative performance indeed of the macro model for these three groups. This finding is supported by the largeMAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$estimates, which are in most cases around or higher than 40% of the corresponding average risk premium. Indeed, theMAPE∗/MARP$MAPE^{*}/MARP$ratio is nearly 60% in the case of GB6; even in the case of M10 (the portfolio group in which the model performs better) such ratio is 31%. It would be difficult to reach these conclusions by relying exclusively on the evidence provided in LM (see their table V), as they do not report aggregate pricing performance metrics (such asROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$andMAPE$MAPE$) for the individual portfolio groups. In addition, the individual and average pricing errors reported in their table V are understated as they do not include the intercept estimates. In sum, the conclusion from the analysis of this subsection is straightforward: relaxing the zero excess zero-beta rate restriction does not benefit LM's model if we appropriately interpret and address the intercept estimates and associated pricing errors.
  The results for the alternative news model (HNI), which are tabulated and discussed in theInternet Appendix, indicate weaker performance relative to the benchmark model. Critically, the null hypothesis—that the 51HNI$HNI$betas are either jointly equal to zero or constant—is not rejected, implying that the respective risk price is not identified (in both cross-sectional regressions). Therefore, all of the asset pricing results associated with that model are doubtful.
article.III. Single-Group Tests:
The analysis conducted in the previous section forces each model to price all portfolio groups on a joint basis. This represents a sensible goal, as any successful asset pricing model should explain all assets, at least conceptually. However, segmented financial markets and other frictions might imply that the factors that price a set of assets (e.g., SBM25) do not coincide with the factors that price another set of assets (e.g., M10). Moreover, one might be interested in assessing the capacity of a model to explain a given group (e.g., SBM25) on an “unconditional” basis, that is, without forcing the model to explain the other groups (e.g., M10, IND10, and GB6) as well. Such evidence would provide empirical support for the model, even if it fails the more challenging task of simultaneously pricing all groups. In the current context, such single-group estimations are likely to clarify the pricing role ofNI$NI$for M10, as the insignificant risk price estimate (discussed in the previous section) is likely to be due to the inclusion of the other groups in the estimation.22
  I start with the zero-intercept case. The results reported in TableI, Panel A indicate that the estimates ofλNI$\lambda _{NI}$are insignificant (at the 5% level) in most cases, the sole exception being the estimation with M10. Further, the rank tests (tabulated in theInternet Appendix) point to a nonrejection of the null (of zero betas) in the estimation with SBM25 or IND10, which means that the macro risk price is not identified. The performance metrics tabulated in theInternet Appendixindicate very negative performance for SBM25, IND10, and GB6 when the risk price estimate is group-specific, asROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$is negative in all three cases. Furthermore, theMAPE$MAPE$is higher than the corresponding CAPM estimate in the estimation with IND10 or GB6. Such evidence is qualitatively similar to that obtained in TableII, Panel A, which suggests that allowing risk prices to change across subsamples does not help the model.23
  I consider alternative testing portfolios in the asset pricing tests, which are associated with other nine prominent market anomalies. As discussed in theInternet Appendix, the implication of the results is clear: the NI model cannot explain these additional portfolio groups, including several momentum-based anomalies.
  I estimate next the macro models on the subsamples by allowing for a nonzero intercept. The results displayed in TableI, Panel B, show that the estimate ofλNI$\lambda _{NI}$becomes significant (5% level) in the estimation with SBM25 (compared to an insignificant estimate in the corresponding zero-intercept specification). However, according to the corresponding rank test reported in theInternet Appendix, one cannot reject the null that such risk price is not identified (p$p$-value of 0.24). Moreover, the corresponding estimate ofα0$\alpha _{0}$is as large as 0.54% per month, significant at the 5% level. Such an implausible estimate would imply an equity premium of only 0.6% per year and an associated pricing error for the market factor as large as 6.36%. In the estimation with IND10, the intercept estimate assumes an even greater magnitude (0.65% per month) with strong statistical significance (1% level), thus implying an even lower aggregate equity premium. Turning to GB6, the excess zero-beta rate estimate is around 0.08% and also strongly significant (1% level). Such an estimate is large relative to the (cross-sectional) average bond risk premium of 0.11% reported in theInternet Appendix. In fact, the corresponding CAPM estimate ofα0$\alpha _{0}$is half the magnitude (0.04%).
  The evaluation metrics under the unrestricted zero-beta rate case are tabulated in theInternet Appendix. By construction, and in contrast to the scenario observed in TableII, Panel B, theROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$estimates are now nonnegative in all cases, including the three “hard-to-price” groups. Interestingly, the macro model now produces a sample explanatory ratio close to 70% for GB6, yet this point estimate is not significant (empiricalp$p$-value well above 10%). In the estimation with the 45 equity portfolios, the explanatory ratio of 42% is significant according to the empiricalp$p$-value of 0.02, but theF$F$-test on the betas (p$p$-value of 0.08) indicates that such fit is questionable. Indeed, a large sampleROLS2$R_{OLS}^{2}$can provide a (very) false impression of a strong performance whenα0$\alpha _{0}$is unrestricted. The average pricing error estimates provide another perspective on the negative performance of the model for the three problematic groups. In the estimation with SBM25 or IND10, theMAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$estimates are around or above 0.54% per month (6.5% per year), which represents respectively 69% and 100% of the respective average raw risk premium. In the estimation with GB6, theMAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$is about 0.08%, which represents 76% of the average bond risk premium. Even in the estimation with M10,MAPE∗$MAPE^{*}$represents a significant fraction (35%) of the correspondingMARP$MARP$. Those estimates are very large in economic terms and represent a clear failure of the model.
article.IV. Conclusion:
LM claim that their macro risk factor—innovation to an index of macro news coverage (NI$NI$)—does a good job explaining cross-sectional dispersion in risk premia associated with 51 testing portfolios related to four distinct groups. Using LM's data, I show that their claims are not accurate: the NI model succeeds in pricing the momentum deciles, but is unable to explain the three remaining portfolio groups and underperforms the factor model of Carhart (1997). On the other hand, the risk price for the alternative macro factor (HNI$HNI$) is not identified, which raises concerns about the validity of the corresponding estimates and associated pricing errors.
  Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon
